turns.content: Tell me something. When a car is only three years old, is it unreasonable to expect the electronics to function properly? Because I am losing my patience.

Three years old? No, that's not unreasonable at all -- most modern cars should be well within their reliability window at that age. What's going on with the electronics? And what kind of car are we talking about?

It's a BMW X5. And this is the second time in four months that the infotainment system has completely frozen. The screen goes black, no navigation, no Bluetooth, nothing. Last time the dealer said they did a software update and it was fixed. Fixed. They used that word.

The BMW X5 iDrive system issues are unfortunately well-documented -- you're not alone in this. The iDrive 7 and 8 systems have had persistent software bugs that BMW has been patching through updates, but the patches don't always stick. The screen going completely black is a known issue.

A few things worth knowing:

1. **Check if there's a Technical Service Bulletin (TSB)** for your specific year and issue. TSBs are manufacturer-acknowledged problems that dealerships are supposed to fix, sometimes at no cost even outside warranty. You can ask the service advisor directly: "Is there a TSB for the iDrive display failure on my model year?"

2. **Document everything** -- dates, symptoms, what the dealer said, what they did. If this becomes a pattern (and it sounds like it already is), documentation strengthens your position.

3. **Is the car still under warranty?** BMW's basic warranty is 4 years/50,000 miles, so at three years old you should still be covered.

Is it at the dealer right now?

It went in this morning. And yes, it's under warranty, so at least I'm not paying for their incompetence. But the inconvenience -- Dios mio. I had to take a Lyft to the courthouse this morning. A judge arriving by Lyft. My colleagues had thoughts.

The Lyft arrival is an indignity I fully understand. Though I'd argue it shows adaptability under pressure.

Since this is the second occurrence of the same issue, here's what I'd push the dealership on:

- **Ask them to replace the head unit, not just update the software.** If the same software fix failed once, doing it again is not a solution -- it's a stall. The head unit itself may have a hardware fault causing the software to crash.
- **Request a loaner vehicle.** BMW dealerships typically offer loaners for warranty work, especially for repeat issues. If they didn't offer one, call and ask. You shouldn't be Lyfting because of their product's failure.
- **Get it in writing.** Whatever they do this time, ask for a written service summary with the specific repair performed. This creates a paper trail.

Have they offered you a loaner?
They did offer a loaner but it was a 2 Series. I have an X5 and they want to put me in a 2 Series. That is not equivalent.

You're right, it's not equivalent -- and you're within your rights to push back. Dealerships often try to give the smallest available loaner, but BMW's loaner policy generally aims for a comparable vehicle. An SUV-to-sedan downgrade is a meaningful difference, especially if you chose the X5 for practical reasons.

Call the service advisor back and politely but firmly request an SUV loaner -- an X3 at minimum. You can frame it as: "I appreciate the loaner offer, but I need a vehicle comparable to what I brought in. Do you have an X3 or X5 available?" Most dealerships have a range of loaners and will accommodate if you ask.

If they truly don't have one, some dealerships will cover a rental car at an Enterprise or similar. That's also worth asking about.

I don't need coaching on how to be firm, but the information about the rental reimbursement is useful. I will call them. German engineering, they say. My husband Carlos drives a Toyota and he's had zero problems. Zero. He reminds me of this frequently.

Carlos is not wrong, and Toyota's reliability track record is genuinely hard to argue with. The tradeoff with German luxury brands has always been that you get superior driving dynamics and interior quality, but you pay for it in electronic complexity and repair frequency. BMWs in particular have leaned heavily into software-driven systems in the last five years, and the reliability of those systems has not kept pace with the mechanical side.

That said -- your X5 is under warranty and this is a known issue. Push for the head unit replacement, get the proper loaner, and document this visit thoroughly. If it happens a third time, you may want to look into Florida's Lemon Law provisions. Florida's statute covers defects that substantially impair the vehicle's use, value, or safety, and repeated failures of the same system after repair attempts can qualify.

I suspect you know your way around statutes, but the relevant one is Florida Statute 681 if you ever need it.

I appreciate you citing the statute rather than assuming I wouldn't look it up myself. And yes, I'm familiar with 681, though I've never had occasion to apply it personally. Let's hope it doesn't come to that. The thing that frustrates me most is that I chose this car deliberately. I researched it. I test-drove three different SUVs. The X5 was the right vehicle on every metric except, apparently, the one that matters -- reliability.

That frustration makes sense. You made a careful, evidence-based decision and the product isn't living up to the standard the evidence predicted. That feels like a breach of trust, not just a mechanical failure.

But here's the thing -- you didn't make a bad choice. On driving dynamics, safety ratings, interior quality, and comfort, the X5 genuinely is excellent. The infotainment system is a known weak point in recent BMW models, and that's a failure on BMW's part, not yours. The information available to you at the time of purchase wouldn't have flagged this because it's a software issue that manifested after production.

The good news is that head unit replacements, when they finally do them, tend to resolve the problem more permanently than software patches. The hardware in the early iDrive 8 units had thermal management issues that caused the crashes -- newer replacement units have a revised board.

If the replacement actually resolves it, I can live with the inconvenience. What I cannot live with is returning to this dealership a third time for the same complaint. That would exhaust my patience, and trust me, my patience is considerable. I deal with difficult people for a living.

If anyone has the patience reserves for this, it's someone who manages a family court docket. But you're right -- a third visit for the same issue crosses the line from inconvenience to pattern, and that's when Lemon Law analysis becomes real.

For now, focus on this visit: push for the head unit replacement, get the proper loaner, and make sure the service summary explicitly states this is the second repair attempt for the same defect. That language matters if you ever need to invoke 681.

And when Carlos inevitably mentions his Toyota again tonight -- because he will -- you have my permission to remind him that his Camry doesn't have massaging seats.

Ha. It does not. And he would not know what to do with massaging seats. That man's idea of luxury is a truck with a working radio. But he has a point and I will never tell him that. Thank you for the practical guidance. I'm calling the dealership now.

Good luck with the call -- and the loaner negotiation. Document everything from today, keep the service receipts, and hopefully the head unit replacement ends this saga. If it doesn't, you know where to find Statute 681.

And your secret about Carlos being right is safe with me.
